original_abstract:
In our continuous search for potent anti-diabetic agents, we have successfully synthesized a series of novel bis-Schiff base derivatives based on a thiobarbituric acid scaffold through a multi-step reaction protocol. Following structural elucidation using advanced spectroscopic techniques, the entire library was evaluated for in vitro α-glucosidase inhibitory potential. Notably, compounds 8 (IC50 = 0.10 ± 0.05 µM) and 9 (IC50 = 0.13 ± 0.03 µM) emerged as the most potent inhibitors, significantly outperforming the commercial standard, acarbose (IC50 = 0.27 ± 0.04 µM). Other derivatives including 5, 6, 7, 10, and 4 also demonstrated substantial inhibitory activity, with IC50 values ranging from 0.32 ± 0.03 to 0.52 ± 0.02 µM. 

Molecular docking simulations were performed to visualize the binding landscape within the target protein, revealing the key residues and stabilizing interactions that drive enzyme inhibition. To gain deeper insight into the electronic properties and chemical stability of these molecules, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted at the B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level. Furthermore, the presence and nature of intramolecular hydrogen bonding were investigated using DFT-D3 and Reduced Density Gradient (RDG) analysis. Finally, time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) at the CAM-B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level was utilized to determine the reactivity parameters of the most promising candidates, providing a comprehensive computational rationale for their observed biological activity.
humanized_abstract:
A novel class of thiobarbituric acid derived bis-Schiff base analogues as α-glucosidase inhibitors Following our persistent efforts towards the development of highly efficacious anti-diabetic drugs, a series of novel thiobarbituric acid derived bis-Schiff base derivatives have been synthesized via a multi-step route. All the synthesized compounds were unequivocally characterized using multi-nuclear NMR as well as various mass spectrometry techniques. These derived compounds were assessed for their in vitro α-glucosidase inhibition activity and the derived IC50 values of compounds 8 and 9 are 0.10 ± 0.05 µM and 0.13 ± 0.03 µM respectively. The IC50 values are remarkably higher for the benchmark acarbose drug, having an IC50 of 0.27 ± 0.04 µM. On the other hand, derived compounds such as 5, 6, 7, 10 as well as 4 displayed moderate α-glucosidase inhibition activities having IC50 values in the range of 0.32 ± 0.03 – 0.52 ± 0.02 µM.

Molecular docking simulations were done in order to describe the interaction profile for each target and thereby the key interactions and amino acids involved for enzymes inhibition. DFT calculations at the B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level have been carried out in order to elucidate electronic characteristics and the stability of the molecules and hence intramolecular hydrogen bond existence were characterized using the DFT-D3 and Reduced Density Gradient (RDG) methods. On the other hand, by the aid of time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) at CAM-B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level the reactivity parameters of the best candidates have been elucidated to gain a complete computational explanation for their interesting bioactivity.

On the other hand, derived compounds such as 5, 6, 7, 10 as well as 4 displayed moderate α-glucosidase inhibition activities having IC50 values in the range of 0.32 ± 0.03 – 0.52 ± 0.02 µM.

Molecular docking simulations were done in order to describe the interaction profile for each target and thereby the key interactions and amino acids involved for enzymes inhibition. DFT calculations at the B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level have been carried out in order to elucidate electronic characteristics and the stability of the molecules and hence intramolecular hydrogen bond existence were characterized using the DFT-D3 and Reduced Density Gradient (RDG) methods.
